item.title: 【Your guide to GrafanaCON 2026 in Barcelona】巴塞罗那 GrafanaCON 2026 指南
item.description:
【

Oh my Gaudi, GrafanaCON 2026 in Barcelona is almost here. 

Join us 20–22 April for our largest community conference that celebrates Grafana and its extended open source ecosystem, including the standards, projects, and people that create our vibrant community. There will be technical deep dives from Google, LEGO, and more, alongside hands-on demos of the latest AI tools. Plus the GrafanaCON Science Fair is back with new hackathon projects to try out. (Did someone say ping-pong?)

Haven’t registered yet? There’s still time. Group discounts start at 20% for two people and go up to 40% for groups of five or more.

If you're already registered, we can't wait to see you. Here’s how to make the most of your GrafanaCON experience.

Step 1: Confirm your ticket and travel plans

Before you arrive, check your inbox for your ticket confirmation from events@grafana.com. No need to print anything; just show your email on your device.

The event is at Palau de Congressos de Catalunya, Av. Diagonal 661, Les Corts, 08028 Barcelona. Transit and venue details are in the FAQ. If you need a place to stay, there are still rooms available at a discounted rate at the Torre Melina, a Gran Meliá Hotel, which is a two-minute walk from the conference. Book here. 

Pro tip: Badge pickup officially opens the day before the conference starts on Monday, 20 April, 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m. Come early to avoid the lines and you'll be ready when the main program opens Tuesday with the opening keynote at 9:00 a.m.

Step 2: Plan your one-of-a-kind conference experience

With 30+ sessions, Ask the Experts, the Science Fair, Birds of a Feather, and a community party, there’s something for everyone. Bookmark the full GrafanaCON agenda for the complete schedule. Here are some of the highlights:

Hands-on labs

Monday is dedicated to hands-on labs led by Grafana Labs engineers. We have a few slots still open through 16 April for the following labs: 

  • OpenTelemetry: Send metrics, logs, and traces to the open source Grafana LGTM Stack 
  • Actually Useful AI: Use AI to build a Grafana plugin that uses AI
  • Alloy: Telemetry pipelines with an OpenTelemetry-style collector and Prometheus pipelines

Hands-on labs require a separate ticket in addition to a regular conference pass. For more details, check out the Hands-on labs page. 

Technical deep dives and community-led sessions

The conference kicks off on Tuesday at 9:00 a.m. with the opening keynote, where Grafana Labs Co-founder and CEO Raj Dutt and Co-founder and CGO Torkel Ödegaard and the Grafana Labs engineering team will reveal major announcements around our open source projects, AI developments, and, of course, we'll unveil what’s new in Grafana 13. (P.S. The keynote is livestreamed so teammates back home can watch.)

Over the two-day GrafanaCON conference, there will be more than 30 sessions where you can check out the latest updates in open source observability and learn from real use cases from the wider community. Here's a glimpse of what's in store: 

Sessions from Grafana Labs 

  • Grafana 13 deep dive: Operate at scale, flexible dashboards, faster answers  
  • Inside Loki’s new architecture: Log insights at petabyte scale  
  • Alloy’s OpenTelemetry engine: Collector YAML and native OTel pipelines in Grafana  
  • Scaling Grafana Assistant: Give AI agents the right context so teams can move faster from signals to action in Grafana  
  • Grafana Alerting: One unified engine and multi-team notification policies
  • k6 2.0: What’s new for load testing and reliability  

Sessions from the community 

  • Google: Less toil, stronger incident response, planet-scale dashboards  
  • The LEGO Group: Observability when platform teams empower builders  
  • DIGI4ECO: Oceans, robotics, AI, and Grafana for restoration telemetry  
  • Irish Rail: A 180-year-old railway on how it modernized with an open monitoring platform

Plus Drive Terra, Python Software Foundation, ESNet, and more! Check out the GrafanaCON agenda for a full rundown of all the talks. 

In addition, be sure to check out:

  • The Golden Grot Awards: We’ll showcase the top three dashboards in each category (professional and personal), and celebrate the grand prize winners on stage.

The hallway track

There's also a lot to take part in outside of the main stage. This is when you get to connect with Grafana Labs experts and community members and where that napkin sketch may transform into your next PR. 

  • Science Fair: Play with popular projects from the community and our annual Grafana Labs hackathons, ranging from monitoring 3D printers to tracking ping-pong rallies.
  • Ask the Experts: Get your questions answered by the engineers and pros behind Grafana, Loki, Mimir, Tempo, Alloy, k6, and more.
  • Birds of a Feather: Drop into informal yet impactful small-group conversations around timely topics, including Alloy and OpenTelemetry, actually useful AI, alerting, diversity and allyship in tech, and more.
  • The community party: Join us on Tuesday, 21 April at 6:00 p.m. for an evening of food, drinks, and conversations with fellow attendees. Badge required. 

And for those who just may need to take a break from all the action (or need to check emails or Slack), there will also be quiet spaces throughout the venue to recharge on both conference days. 

Step 3: Final tips to keep in mind

We aim to make the GrafanaCON a positive experience for all attendees. Our Code of Conduct ensures a safe, inclusive space for everyone — reach out to report-conduct@grafana.com or find our on-site staff to learn more.

If you and your team are still interested in attending, there are group discounts available in the final week leading up to the conference with up to 40% tickets for group of 5 or more. Check out the GrafanaCON site for more information. 

We'll see you in Barcelona.

】

哦我的高迪，巴塞罗那 GrafanaCON 2026 即将到来。 

4 月 20 日至 22 日与我们一起参加我们最大的社区会议，庆祝 Grafana 及其扩展的开源生态系统，包括创建我们充满活力的社区的标准、项目和人员。届时将有来自谷歌、乐高等公司的技术深入探讨，以及最新人工智能工具的实践演示。此外，GrafanaCON Science Fair 又回来了，并带来了新的黑客马拉松项目可供尝试。 （有人说乒乓球吗？）

还没报名？还有时间。两人团体折扣起价为 20%，五人或以上团体折扣高达 40%。

如果您已经注册，我们迫不及待地想见到您。以下是如何充分利用您的 GrafanaCON 体验。

第 1 步：确认您的门票和旅行计划

在抵达之前，请检查您的收件箱，查看来自 events@grafana.com 的门票确认信息。无需打印任何内容；只需在设备上显示您的电子邮件即可。

活动地点为加泰罗尼亚国会宫 (Palau de Congressos de Catalunya)，Av.对角线 661，勒科尔茨，08028 巴塞罗那。交通和场地详情请参阅常见问题解答。如果您需要住宿，Torre Melina, a Gran Meliá Hotel 酒店仍然提供折扣价的客房，距离会议中心有 2 分钟步行路程。 此处预订。 

专业提示：徽章领取在会议开始前一天（4 月 20 日星期一）上午 8:00 至下午 5:00 正式开放。早点来以避免排队，当周二主要活动于上午 9:00 开始并举行开幕主题演讲时，您就可以准备好了。

第 2 步：规划您独一无二的会议体验

30 多场会议，请教专家、科学博览会 物以类聚以及社区聚会，每个人都能找到适合自己的东西。将完整的 GrafanaCON 议程添加为书签，以获取完整的日程安排。以下是一些亮点：

动手实验室

周一专门举办由 Grafana Labs 工程师领导的动手实验室。截至 4 月 16 日，以下实验室仍有一些开放时间：

  • OpenTelemetry：将指标、日志和跟踪发送到开源 Grafana LGTM 堆栈
  • 真正有用的 AI：使用 AI 构建使用 AI 的 Grafana 插件
  • Alloy：具有 OpenTelemetry 风格收集器和 Prometheus 管道的遥测管道

除了常规会议通行证之外，动手实验还需要单独的门票。有关更多详细信息，请查看动手实验室页面。 

技术深入探讨和社区主导的会议

会议于周二上午 9:00 以开幕主题演讲拉开帷幕，Grafana Labs 联合创始人兼首席执行官 Raj Dutt、联合创始人兼 CGO Torkel Ödegaard 以及 Grafana Labs 工程团队将在会上发布有关以下方面的重大公告我们的开源项目、人工智能开发，当然，我们还将揭晓 Grafana 13 的新功能。（注：主题演讲是直播的，以便家乡的队友可以观看。）

在为期两天的 GrafanaCON 会议上，将举行 30 多场会议，您可以在其中查看开源可观察性的最新更新，并从更广泛的社区的真实用例中学习。以下是即将推出的内容的一瞥：

Grafana Labs 的会议 

  • Grafana 13 深入探讨：大规模运营、灵活的仪表板、更快的答案  
  • Loki 的新架构内部：PB 级日志洞察  
  • Alloy 的 OpenTelemetry 引擎：收集器 YAML 和本机 OTel 管道Grafana  
  • 扩展 Grafana 助手：为 AI 代理提供正确的上下文，以便团队可以在 Grafana 中更快地从信号转向行动  
  • Grafana警报：一个统一的引擎和多团队通知策略
  • k6 2.0：负载测试和可靠性方面的新变化  

社区会议

  • Google：更少的劳作、更强的事件响应、全球规模的仪表板  
  • 乐高集团：平台团队为构建者提供支持时的可观察性  
  • DIGI4ECO：海洋、机器人、人工智能和 Grafana 用于恢复遥测  
  • 爱尔兰铁路：一条拥有 180 年历史的铁路如何实现现代化具有开放的监控平台

加上 Drive Terra、Python Software Foundation、ESNet 等等！查看 GrafanaCON 议程，了解所有会谈的完整摘要。 

此外，请务必查看：

  • Golden Grot 奖：我们将展示每个类别（专业和个人）的前三名仪表板，并在舞台上庆祝大奖获得者。

走廊轨道

主舞台之外还有很多可以参与。这是您与 Grafana Labs 专家和社区成员联系的时候，餐巾纸草图可能会在其中出现转变为您的下一个 PR。

  • Science Fair：参与社区和我们年度 Grafana Labs 黑客马拉松的热门项目，从监控 3D 打印机到跟踪乒乓球比赛。
  • 询问专家：让 Grafana、Loki、Mimir、Tempo、Alloy、k6 等背后的工程师和专业人士解答您的问题。
  • 物以类聚：围绕及时的主题进行非正式但有影响力的小组对话，包括合金和 OpenTelemetry、实际有用的人工智能、警报、技术领域的多样性和盟友关系等等。
  • 社区聚会：于 4 月 21 日星期二加入我们，网址为：下午 6:00，需要入场券并与其他与会者交谈。

对于那些可能需要在所有活动中休息一下（或需要查看电子邮件或 Slack）的人来说，会议期间，整个会场都会有安静的空间供您充电。

第 3 步：要记住的最后提示

我们的目标是让 GrafanaCON 成为一个积极的活动我们的行为准则确保为每个人提供一个安全、包容的空间 - 请联系report-conduct@grafana.com 或联系我们的现场工作人员了解更多信息。

如果您和您的团队仍有兴趣参加，会议前最后一周可享受团体折扣，5 人或以上团体可享受高达 40% 的门票优惠，请查看GrafanaCON 网站了解更多信息。

我们在巴塞罗那见。

item.title: 【Grafana Alerting: Respond faster and get situational awareness with alert enrichment in Grafana Cloud】Grafana 警报：通过 Grafana Cloud 中的警报丰富功能更快地响应并获得态势感知
item.description:
【

Alerts are meant to help teams respond quickly to problems, but too often they arrive without enough context to be immediately useful. 

An alert that says “CPU usage is high” still leaves the on-call engineer asking critical follow-up questions: Which service? Which environment? Where do I look next? Validating the alert and triaging the situation is the first step for every engineer. It's a manual step that takes time, extending every potential incident.

Grafana Alerting addresses this gap with alert enrichment, a new feature in public preview for Grafana Cloud users that lets you attach meaningful, actionable context to alerts before they reach responders. 

With enrichment, alerts are more than just signals; they're entry points into investigation and resolution. Enrichments automate the first actions of responding engineers, allowing them to focus on the potential incident rather than spending time gathering data. Triage starts when the notification is sent.

What is alert enrichment in Grafana Alerting?

Prior to this update, Grafana alert notifications were limited to showing information obtained from the query itself. This means the information you want to present has to be obtainable from the expressions in the query and incorporated into the result. It’s a severe limitation on what exactly can be added to the resultant notification, and can also result in overly complex queries that are slow and expensive to run on every evaluation interval.

This is where enrichment comes into play. Alert enrichment is the practice of adding contextual information to alerts. This could be in the form of additional labels, annotations, and even log lines. This context travels with the alert all the way through to notification delivery.

Enrichment can include:

  • Log lines relevant to the firing alert containing error messages
  • Human-readable summaries and descriptions
  • Links to dashboards and logs
  • A Grafana Assistant AI investigation
  • Links to Grafana services that can aid in triaging an alert (Assistant, Knowledge Graph)

When you're dealing with real incidents, this type of functionality really matters. It isn’t about making alerts “prettier,” it’s about adding the right context so it's operationally effective. The difference between a noisy alert and a useful one often comes down to enrichment.

Making enrichment a standard practice

The real power of alert enrichment in Grafana Alerting comes from treating it as a baseline requirement, not an optional extra. A well-enriched alert should answer, at a glance:

  • What's happening
  • Where it's happening
  • Who owns it
  • What you should do next

Here's a list of available enrichments:

  • Assign: Allows the addition of alert annotations, or even the modification of existing ones. This enricher also supports templating so you can add computed values from labels or format alert descriptions dynamically (e.g., Alert {{ $labels.alertname }} on instance {{ $labels.instance }})
  • External: Calls out to an external webhook or API. This allows integration with external services to fetch your own additional context outside the Grafana ecosystem
  • Datasource: Runs a Grafana data source query. It can pull related logs from Loki, fetch metrics from Mimir, or query any configured Grafana data source to fetch additional context. 
  • Sift: Triggers a Grafana Sift ML investigation. This automatically starts an ML-powered investigation and adds a link to the Sift investigation dashboard. Learn more about Sift here.
  • Knowledge Graph: Calls the knowledge graph for automated root cause analysis and adds a Workbench link to the alert. Learn more about Knowledge Graph here.
  • Explain: Adds an annotation with an AI-powered alert explanation. It uses Grafana’s LLM plugin to generate human-readable explanations with likely causes and suggested next steps.
  • Assistant: Triggers an Assistant investigation and adds an annotation with a link to the Grafana Assistant Investigation report. Learn more about Assistant Investigations here.

Before vs. after: the impact of alert enrichment

To understand why alert enrichment matters, it helps to compare what responders actually see before and after enrichment is applied.

Before: An unenriched alert

The screenshot above shows a regular alert. There isn’t much information there apart from the cluster and namespace that triggered the alert. It’s not immediately clear for a user where to go next: it might be searching through logs, checking some metrics, or opening a dashboard. All this context switching costs precious minutes, especially during an incident.

After: an enriched Grafana alert

This is the exact same alert, except it has been supercharged with multiple enrichments. Links to Assistant and Sift have been added, offering users a click-through to AI-powered investigations that were triggered in the background. 

There are two annotations assigned via an "Assign" enrichment for demonstration purposes:

  • foo=bar
  • tmpl={{ $labels.alertname }} is firing on {{ .Labels.k8s_cluster_name }}. As seen above, this template has been executed before being added as an annotation value.

The "Explain" enrichment has added a human-readable explanation of likely causes under the “AI Explanation” header. 

Finally, the "Datasource" enrichment has pulled in relevant log lines, as well as a link to explore these logs in further detail. From the logs, the issue is immediately apparent, there is a timeout from the payment provider. Now the user knows exactly what the problem is directly from the alert itself and can concentrate on mitigating the issue, rather than spending valuable time triaging.

How to get started?

Rule enrichments

Getting started with configuring your own enrichments couldn’t be easier. On the rule page, you will find a new tab called "Alert enrichment":

From here, you can configure the enrichment of your choice by selecting the “Add alert enrichment” dropdown:

Once saved, the enrichment will run every time for that rule before alert delivery. In the enrichment shown above, an alert for that rule will contain the log lines from the query specified, as well an Explore link taking you directly to the query to view the logs in more detail.

Global enrichments

For users with Admin privileges, the "Alert Enrichment" tab is visible under Alerting > Settings. From here, an enrichment can be configured to run on all alerts, or alternatively scope them by labels or annotations. In the following example, the enrichment would only run for all alerts containing the label foo=bar.

You can find more info on how to get started on setting up alert enrichments in our docs.  

Do you use the Grafana Assistant?

Grafana Assistant is already capable of listing enrichments, as well as creating and deleting a few types such as Assistant enrichments, which will trigger an investigation, and Datasource:Loki enrichments.

We plan on supporting more enrichment types via Grafana Assistant in the near future.

】

警报旨在帮助团队快速响应问题，但警报往往没有足够的背景信息而无法立即发挥作用。 

“CPU 使用率过高”的警报仍然让值班工程师提出关键的后续问题：哪项服务？哪个环境？接下来我该看哪里？验证警报并对情况进行分类是每个工程师的第一步。这是一个需要时间的手动步骤，会延长每个潜在事件的时间。

Grafana Alerting 通过警报丰富解决了这一差距，这是 Grafana Cloud 用户公共预览版中的一项新功能，可让您将有意义的、可操作的上下文附加到警报中在他们到达响应者之前。 

通过丰富，警报不再只是信号；而是信号。它们是调查和解决问题的切入点。丰富功能使响应工程师的第一个行动自动化，使他们能够专注于潜在的事件，而不是花时间收集数据。发送通知时开始分类。

什么是 Grafana 警报中的警报丰富？

在此更新之前，Grafana 警报通知仅限于显示从查询本身获取的信息。这意味着您想要呈现的信息必须可以从查询中的表达式中获取并合并到结果中。这对于到底可以添加到结果通知中的内容存在严格限制，并且还可能导致过于复杂的查询，这些查询在每个评估间隔上运行速度缓慢且成本高昂。

这就是丰富性发挥作用的地方。警报丰富是向警报添加上下文信息的做法。这可以是附加标签、注释甚至日志行的形式。此上下文随警报一起传递，直至通知传递。

丰富内容可以包括：

  • 与包含错误消息的触发警报相关的日志行
  • 人类可读的摘要和描述
  • 仪表板和日志的链接
  • A Grafana助理 AI 调查
  • 指向 Grafana 服务的链接，可帮助对警报进行分类（助理、知识图）

当您处理真实事件时，此类功能确实很重要。这并不是要让警报“更漂亮”，而是要添加正确的上下文，使其在操作上有效。嘈杂的警报和有用的警报之间的区别通常取决于丰富性。

使丰富性成为标准实践

Grafana Alerting 中警报丰富性的真正力量来自于将其视为基线要求，而不是可选的额外要求。内容丰富的警报应该一目了然地回答：

  • 发生了什么
  • 发生在哪里
  • 谁拥有它
  • 接下来应该做什么

以下是可用丰富功能的列表：

  • 分配：允许添加警报注释，甚至可以修改现有注释。此丰富器还支持模板化，因此您可以动态添加标签计算值或格式化警报描述（例如，实例 {{ $labels.instance }} 上的 Alert {{ $labels.alertname }}）
  • 外部： 调用外部 Webhook 或 API。这允许与外部服务集成，以在 Grafana 生态系统之外获取您自己的附加上下文
  • 数据源：运行 Grafana 数据源查询。它可以从 Loki 提取相关日志，从 Mimir 获取指标，或查询任何配置的 Grafana 数据源以获取其他上下文。 
  • Sift：触发 Grafana Sift ML 调查。这会自动启动 ML 支持的调查，并添加指向 Sift 调查仪表板的链接。 此处了解有关 Sift 的更多信息。
  • 知识图：调用知识图进行自动根本原因分析，并向警报添加工作台链接。 此处了解有关知识图的更多信息。
  • 说明：添加带有 AI 驱动的警报说明的注释。它使用 Grafana 的 LLM 插件生成人类可读的解释，其中包含可能的原因和建议的后续步骤。
  • 助理：触发助理调查并添加带有 Grafana 助理调查报告链接的注释。 此处了解有关助理调查的更多信息。

前后对比：警报丰富化的影响

要了解警报丰富化为何如此重要，有助于比较响应者在丰富化之前和之后实际看到的内容

之前：未丰富的警报

上面的屏幕截图显示了常规警报。除了触发警报的集群和命名空间之外，没有太多信息。用户并不清楚下一步该去哪里：可能是搜索日志、检查一些指标或打开仪表板。所有这些上下文切换都会花费宝贵的时间，尤其是在发生事件期间。

之后：丰富的 Grafana 警报

这是完全相同的警报，只不过它已通过多种丰富内容进行了增强。添加了 Assistant 和 Sift 的链接，让用户可以点击进入后台触发的人工智能调查。 

出于演示目的，通过“分配”丰富分配了两个注释：

  • foo=bar
  • tmpl={{ $labels.alertname }} 正在 {{ .Labels.k8s_cluster_name }} 上触发。如上所示，该模板在添加为注释值之前已被执行。

“Explain”丰富功能在“AI Explanation”标题下添加了对可能原因的人类可读的解释。 

最后，“数据源”丰富了相关日志行，以及用于更详细地探索这些日志的链接。从日志来看，问题很明显，支付提供商超时。现在，用户可以直接从警报本身确切地知道问题出在哪里，并且可以集中精力缓解问题，而不是花费宝贵的时间进行分类。

如何开始？

规则丰富

开始配置自己的丰富功能再简单不过了。在规则页面上，您将找到一个名为“警报丰富”的新选项卡：

在这里，您可以通过选择“添加警报丰富”下拉列表来配置您选择的丰富：

保存后，每次在警报传送之前都会针对该规则运行丰富。在上面显示的丰富内容中，该规则的警报将包含指定查询的日志行，以及一个浏览链接，可直接转至该查询以更详细地查看日志。

全局丰富内容

对于具有管理员权限的用户，“警报丰富”选项卡位于警报 > 设置下。从这里，可以将丰富配置为在所有警报上运行，或者通过标签或注释来确定它们的范围。在以下示例中，丰富功能仅针对包含标签 foo=bar 的所有警报运行。

您可以在我们的网站中找到有关如何开始设置警报丰富功能的更多信息。文档。  

您使用 Grafana Assistant 吗？

Grafana Assistant 已经能够列出丰富内容，以及创建和删除一些类型，例如将触发调查的 Assistant 丰富内容以及 Datasource:Loki 丰富内容。

我们计划在不久的将来通过 Grafana Assistant 支持更多丰富类型。

item.title: 【How to manage synthetic monitoring checks as code with Terraform and Grafana Cloud】如何使用 Terraform 和 Grafana Cloud 以代码形式管理综合监控检查
item.description:
【

As teams scale, managing synthetic monitoring checks manually in the UI becomes difficult and error-prone. When you're dealing with dozens of checks across multiple environments, teams experience inconsistent configurations, lack of version control, and difficulty tracking changes. 

This is where managing your Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring checks as code using Terraform can be valuable, allowing you to define checks programmatically, maintain version control, and deploy consistent configurations across environments. This helps you scale your synthetic monitoring strategy while ensuring reproducible setups.

In this blog post, we'll show you how to export a Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring check from the UI, configure the Terraform provider, and deploy and update checks using Terraform. You can also learn more by watching the video below.

Why manage checks as code?

Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring is a blackbox monitoring solution that lets you run a variety checks such as browser checks, HTTP checks, and DNS checks from globally distributed public probes or self-hosted private probes to proactively measure the availability, latency, and correctness of your service.

As your monitoring needs grow beyond a handful of checks, the manual UI-based approach becomes unsustainable. With each change to a check, you risk environments drifting out of sync, and there's no audit trail for modifications. While tools like Grizzly or the Grafana CLI can also help manage checks at scale, Terraform is our recommended approach

Terraform offers mature state management and broad ecosystem support for scalable monitoring operations. Managing checks as code with Terraform provides a consistent, scalable, and repeatable foundation for synthetic monitoring as your system grows, with key benefits including:

  • Version control: Track every change to your monitoring configuration in Git
  • Consistency: Ensure checks are configured identically across dev, staging, and production
  • Collaboration: Review changes through pull requests before deployment
  • Scalability: Deploy and manage hundreds of checks with a single command
  • Reusability: Template similar checks and modify parameters as needed

The Terraform workflow for synthetic monitoring checks

A common Terraform workflow for synthetic monitoring checks looks like this:

1. Prototype in the Grafana UI: Start by creating your check in the UI to validate the configuration, test functionality, and explore available options. This prototyping approach is faster than writing configuration files from scratch.

2. Configure the Terraform provider: This tells Terraform which Grafana Cloud stack to manage.

3. Export the check to Terraform: Once exported, the check becomes a Terraform resource that you can modify and reuse.

4. Deploy and manage updates with Terraform: from this point forward, Terraform becomes the source of truth for modifying and managing checks.

Let's walk through each step.

Prototype in the Grafana UI 

Start by creating your check in the UI. Once it has been created, we will collect our dedicated access token and probe API server URL.


1. In Grafana Cloud, navigate to Synthetic Monitoring and click Config.

2. Click Generate access token and copy the value.

3. Note the API endpoint URL shown on this page, as you'll need it when configuring the Terraform provider.

Configure the Terraform provider

We'll create a terraform.tfvars file to store our credentials:

sm_access_token = "your-synthetic-monitoring-access-token"
sm_url = "https://synthetic-monitoring-api.grafana.net"

Note: The Synthetic Monitoring provider uses a separate URL and access token from the standard Grafana Terraform provider. If you're managing other Grafana resources in the same Terraform workspace, you'll need both configured separately.

Next, we'll configure the provider in main.tf:

With that in place, we can initialize the Terraform provider:

terraform init

Export your check as Terraform HCL

Rather than writing the resource config from scratch, Grafana can generate it for us directly from an existing check.

  1. In Grafana Cloud, go to Synthetic Monitoring and click Config.
  2. Select the Terraform tab and find the check you want to manage.
  3. Choose HCL format and copy the resource block.

The exported HCL will look something like this:

We'll paste this into a checks.tf file. From this point on, Terraform is the source of truth for this check.

Deploy multiple checks

One of the key advantages of managing checks as code is being able to define multiple checks with consistent configuration. Here's an example with two team-specific checks, where one targets a dev environment and one enforces SSL validation for a security team endpoint.

To deploy both checks, we run:

terraform apply

Terraform will display a plan showing exactly what it's about to create and prompt us to confirm before making any changes. Once we approve, the new checks will appear in Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring.

Update checks over time

Once our checks are in Terraform, making updates is straightforward. We edit the relevant fields in the .tf file and run terraform apply again. For example, to update the dev check to run every two minutes and reassign it to the dev-2 team, we update the frequency and label.

This makes it straightforward to review and audit changes, and integrates cleanly into a CI/CD pipeline where terraform plan output can be part of a pull request review process.

Note: Once you move to Terraform, avoid editing checks in the Grafana UI. After the first terraform apply, Terraform owns the state, and any changes made directly in the UI will be overwritten on the next apply.

Wrapping up

Managing synthetic monitoring checks as code with Terraform provides a consistent, scalable, and repeatable foundation for your checks as your system grows. By leveraging Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring with Terraform, you can version, review, and deploy changes to your checks using the same workflows you already use for the rest of your infrastructure.

To learn more about provisioning Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring resources, refer to our technical documentation. For a full Terraform reference on available check settings, including DNS, TCP, ping, and scripted checks, visit the Terraform Registry.

Grafana Cloud is the easiest way to get started with synthetic monitoring. We have a generous free tier that includes 100k test executions per month and more. Sign up for free now!

】

随着团队规模的扩大，在 UI 中手动管理综合监控检查变得困难且容易出错。当您在多个环境中处理数十个检查时，团队会遇到配置不一致、缺乏版本控制以及跟踪更改的困难。 

这就是使用 Terraform 将 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 检查作为代码进行管理的价值所在，它允许您以编程方式定义检查、维护版本控制以及跨环境部署一致的配置。这有助于您扩展综合监控策略，同时确保设置可重复。

在这篇博文中，我们将向您展示如何从 UI 导出 Grafana Cloud 综合监控检查、配置 Terraform 提供程序以及使用 Terraform 部署和更新检查。您还可以通过观看下面的视频了解更多信息。

为什么将检查作为代码进行管理？

Grafana Cloud Synthetic-Monitoring/">Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 是一种黑盒监控解决方案，可让您运行各种检查，例如浏览器检查， HTTP 检查，以及来自全球分布式公共探针或自托管的 DNS 检查 私有探测，用于主动测量服务的可用性、延迟和正确性。

随着您的监控需求超出少数检查范围，基于 UI 的手动方法变得不可持续。每次对检查进行更改时，您都会面临环境不同步的风险，并且没有针对修改的审核跟踪。虽然 Grizzly 或 Grafana CLI 等工具也可以帮助管理大规模检查，Terraform 是我们推荐的方法

Terraform 为可扩展的监控操作提供成熟的状态管理和广泛的生态系统支持。随着系统的发展，使用 Terraform 将检查作为代码进行管理，为综合监控提供了一致、可扩展且可重复的基础，主要优势包括：

  • 版本控制：跟踪 Git 中监控配置的每项更改
  • 一致性：确保在开发、登台和生产中配置检查的方式相同
  • 协作：在部署前通过拉取请求审核更改部署
  • 可扩展性：使用单个命令部署和管理数百个检查
  • 可重用性：模板化类似的检查并根据需要修改参数

用于综合监控检查的 Terraform 工作流程

用于综合监控检查的常见 Terraform 工作流程如下所示：

1. Grafana UI 中的原型：首先在 UI 中创建检查以验证配置、测试功能并探索可用选项。这种原型设计方法比从头开始编写配置文件要快。

2.配置 Terraform 提供程序：这告诉 Terraform 要管理哪个 Grafana Cloud 堆栈。

3.将支票导出到 Terraform：导出后，支票将成为您可以修改和重用的 Terraform 资源。

4.使用 Terraform 部署和管理更新：从现在起，Terraform 成为修改和管理检查的真实来源。

让我们逐步完成每个步骤。

Grafana UI 中的原型

首先在 UI 中创建检查。创建后，我们将收集专用访问令牌并探测 API 服务器 URL。


1.在 Grafana Cloud 中，导航至综合监控，然后单击配置。

2.单击生成访问令牌并复制该值。

3.请注意此页面上显示的 API 端点 URL，因为您在配置 Terraform 提供程序时需要它。

配置 Terraform 提供程序

我们将创建一个 terraform.tfvars 文件来存储我们的凭据：

sm_access_token = "your-synthetic-monitoring-access-token"
sm_url = "https://synthetic-monitoring-api.grafana.net"

注意： 综合监控提供程序使用来自标准 Grafana Terraform 提供程序的单独 URL 和访问令牌。如果您在同一个 Terraform 工作区中管理其他 Grafana 资源，则需要单独配置这两个资源。

接下来，我们将在 main.tf 中配置提供程序：

完成后，我们可以初始化 Terraform 提供程序：

terraform init

将检查导出为 Terraform HCL

而不是编写资源配置从头开始，Grafana 可以直接从现有的检查中为我们生成它。

  1. 在 Grafana Cloud 中，转到综合监控并单击配置。
  2. 选择Terraform选项卡并找到您要管理的检查。
  3. 选择HCL格式并复制资源块。

导出的HCL 看起来像这样：

我们将其粘贴到 checks.tf 文件中。从现在起，Terraform 就是此检查的真实来源。

部署多项检查

在代码中管理检查的关键优势之一是能够e 以一致的配置定义多个检查。下面是一个包含两项特定于团队的检查的示例，其中一项针对开发环境，一项针对安全团队端点强制进行 SSL 验证。

要部署这两项检查，我们运行：

terraform apply

Terraform 将显示一个计划，准确显示它将要创建的内容，并提示我们在进行任何更改之前进行确认。一旦我们批准，新的检查将出现在 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 中。

随着时间的推移更新检查

一旦我们的检查出现在 Terraform 中，更新就很简单。我们编辑 .tf 文件中的相关字段并再次运行 terraform apply。例如，要更新开发检查以每两分钟运行一次并将其重新分配给 dev-2 团队，我们会更新频率和标签。

这使得审查和审核更改变得简单，并干净地集成到 CI/CD 管道中，其中 terraform plan 输出可以成为拉取请求审查流程的一部分。

注意：迁移到 Terraform 后，避免在 Grafana UI 中编辑检查。 terraform apply，Terraform 拥有状态，直接在 UI 中进行的任何更改都将在下一次应用时被覆盖。

总结

使用 Terraform 将综合监控检查作为代码进行管理，为您的检查提供一致、可扩展且可重复的基础，如下所示：你的系统会增长。通过利用 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 和 Terraform，您可以使用与其余基础设施相同的工作流程对检查进行版本控制、审核和部署更改。

要了解有关配置 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 资源的更多信息，请参阅我们的 技术文档。有关可用检查设置（包括 DNS、TCP、ping 和脚本检查）的完整 Terraform 参考，请访问 Terraform 注册表。

Grafana Cloud是开始进行综合监控的最简单方法。我们有一个慷慨的免费套餐，包括每月 10 万次测试执行等等。 立即免费注册！

item.title: 【A faster way to pinpoint performance bottlenecks: Using Profiles Drilldown with Grafana Cloud Knowledge Graph】更快速地查明性能瓶颈的方法：结合使用 Profiles Drilldown 和 Grafana Cloud Knowledge Graph
item.description:
【

When you identify CPU or memory spikes in your services, it’s critical to understand why they’re happening. But switching between tools or crafting complex queries can slow you down when trying to pinpoint a root cause.

This is why we’re excited to share that Profiles Drilldown, an application that lets you easily explore profiling data through an intuitive, point-and-click interface (no queries required), is now integrated with Grafana Cloud Knowledge Graph. 

This powerful integration brings CPU profiling data directly into your existing knowledge graph workflows, making it easier and faster to find the exact code paths responsible for performance bottlenecks.

Why integrate the Drilldown apps with the knowledge graph?

The knowledge graph connects metrics, logs, traces, and profiles across Application Observability, Frontend Observability, and Kubernetes Monitoring solutions in Grafana Cloud and combines the telemetry into a single intelligent map of a system's apps, databases, nodes, and more. By modeling your environment as entities and relationships, the knowledge graph lets you correlate telemetry and investigations across any part of your stack so you can resolve issues faster.

Profiles Drilldown builds on the queryless investigation experience already available in Grafana Cloud Knowledge Graph. With this integration, you can move from infrastructure and application signals into code-level profiling data without leaving your investigation workflow. The goal of bringing Profiles Drilldown into the knowledge graph is simple: to help you go from detecting a performance issue to understanding the exact code paths behind it, without changing context or writing complex queries.

For example, if you’re investigating a service with high CPU or memory usage, you can now open Profiles Drilldown directly from the knowledge graph KPI drawer for that entity, such as a service or pod. Profiles Drilldown automatically inherits the entity context, filters, and time window from your knowledge graph investigation.

In addition to viewing your profiling data from within the knowledge graph drawer, you can also view it alongside Metrics Drilldown, Logs Drilldown, and Traces Drilldown for a fully integrated experience.

This allows for:

  • Faster root cause analysis: Move immediately from spotting a high resource usage alert in the knowledge graph to seeing flame graphs and profiling data that explain why that resource is being consumed.
  • Contextual insight: Profiles Drilldown automatically filters the profiling data based on the knowledge graph entity you are focused on, ensuring you dive straight into the most relevant data.
  • A fully queryless experience: You can connect profiling data to operational context without manually building queries across tools.

A streamlined workflow for troubleshooting 

With Profiles Drilldown embedded in the knowledge graph, troubleshooting performance issues becomes much more direct. Here’s an example of what that new workflow looks like:

  1. Spot the issue in the knowledge graph: Identify an anomalous KPI related to CPU or memory usage.
  2. Access Profiles Drilldown: Click to view the service's profiles directly in the knowledge graph KPI drawer.
  3. Explore the flame graph: Instantly see the flame graph rendered within the knowledge graph, showing the exact functions consuming resources over time.
  4. Isolate the problem: Use the interactive flame graph and top function list to identify the most expensive operations, accelerating your path to a fix.

The embedded experience uses the same intuitive interface as the standalone Profiles Drilldown app, allowing you to quickly switch between CPU and memory profiles, and compare historical trends and attributes without ever leaving the knowledge graph.

Next steps

This integration reflects our on-going commitment to reducing complexity across observability workflows. Grafana Cloud Knowledge Graph helps you identify where a problem is happening, and Profiles Drilldown helps you understand why by exposing the code-level work behind CPU and memory consumption.

To learn more, please check out our documentation for the knowledge graph and Drilldown apps, and stay tuned as we continue to extend our exploration workflows across Grafana Cloud.

Grafana Cloud is the easiest way to get started with metrics, logs, traces, dashboards, and more. We have a generous forever-free tier and plans for every use case. Sign up for free now!

】

当您发现服务中出现 CPU 或内存峰值时，了解其发生的原因至关重要。但是，在尝试查明根本原因时，在工具之间切换或创建复杂的查询可能会减慢您的速度。

这就是为什么我们很高兴与大家分享Profiles Drilldown，该应用程序可让您通过直观的点击式界面（无查询）轻松探索分析数据必需），现已与 Grafana Cloud 知识图集成。 

这种强大的集成将 CPU 分析数据直接引入您现有的知识图工作流程中，从而更轻松、更快速地找到导致性能瓶颈的确切代码路径。

为什么将 Drilldown 应用与知识图集成？

知识图连接了 应用程序可观察性，前端Grafana Cloud 中的可观测性和 Kubernetes 监控解决方案，并将遥测数据合并到系统应用、数据库、节点等的单个智能地图中。通过将您的环境建模为实体和关系，知识图谱可让您将堆栈任何部分的遥测和调查关联起来，以便您可以更快地解决问题。

配置文件深入分析建立在 Grafana Cloud 知识图谱中已有的无查询调查体验的基础上。通过这种集成，您可以从基础设施和应用程序信号转移到代码级分析数据，而无需离开调查工作流程。将 Profiles Drilldown 引入知识图谱的目标很简单：帮助您从检测性能问题转向了解其背后的确切代码路径，而无需更改上下文或编写复杂的查询。

例如，如果您正在调查 CPU 或内存使用率较高的服务，您现在可以直接从该知识图 KPI 抽屉中打开 Profiles Drilldown 实体，例如服务或 Pod。配置文件钻取会自动继承知识图调查中的实体上下文、过滤器和时间窗口。

除了从知识图抽屉中查看分析数据之外，您还可以与指标钻取、日志钻取和跟踪钻取一起查看它lldown 以获得完全集成的体验。

这样可以：

  • 更快的根本原因分析：立即从在知识图中发现高资源使用率警报转向查看火焰图和分析数据，解释资源被消耗的原因。
  • 上下文洞察：配置文件深入分析会根据您关注的知识图实体自动过滤分析数据，确保您直接深入到最相关的数据。
  • 完全无查询的体验：您可以将分析数据连接到操作上下文，而无需跨工具手动构建查询。

简化的数据分析故障排除的工作流程

通过知识图中嵌入的配置文件深入分析，性能问题的故障排除变得更加直接。以下是新工作流程的示例：

  1. 在知识图中发现问题：识别与 CPU 或内存使用相关的异常 KPI。
  2. 访问配置文件深入查看：单击以直接在知识图 KPI 抽屉中查看服务的配置文件。
  3. 探索火焰图：立即查看知识中呈现的火焰图图表，显示随着时间的推移消耗资源的确切函数。
  4. 隔离问题：使用交互式火焰图和顶级函数列表来识别最昂贵的操作，从而加快解决问题的速度。

嵌入式体验使用与独立配置文件深入分析应用程序相同的直观界面，使您可以在 CPU 和内存配置文件之间快速切换，并比较历史趋势和属性，而无需离开知识图谱。

下一步步骤

此集成反映了我们对降低可观察性工作流程复杂性的持续承诺。 Grafana Cloud 知识图可帮助您确定问题发生的位置，Profiles Drilldown 通过揭示 CPU 和内存消耗背后的代码级工作来帮助您了解原因。

要了解更多信息，请查看我们的知识图和深入了解应用，请继续关注我们继续在 Grafana Cloud 上扩展我们的探索工作流程。

Grafana Cloud 是开始使用指标、日志、跟踪、仪表板等的最简单方法。我们为每个用例提供慷慨的永久免费套餐和计划。 注册现在免费！

item.title: 【Kubernetes Monitoring Helm chart v4: Biggest update ever!】Kubernetes 监控 Helm Chart v4：有史以来最大的更新！
item.description:
【

The Kubernetes Monitoring Helm chart is the easiest way to send metrics, logs, traces, and profiles from your Kubernetes clusters to Grafana Cloud (or a self-hosted Grafana stack). And version 4.0 is the biggest update the chart has ever received.

Representing nearly six months of planning and development, it's designed to solve real pain points that users have hit as their monitoring setups have grown. The result is a chart that's more predictable, more flexible, and much easier to maintain, whether you manage one cluster or a hundred.

If terms like "Helm values file" or "DaemonSet" sound intimidating, don't worry. This post walks through every major change in plain language, explains the problem it solves, and shows what the fix looks like in practice.

Destinations

Destinations are where your telemetry data is sent, like a Prometheus server for metrics or a Loki instance for logs.

The issue

In v3, destinations were defined as a list:

# v3
destinations:
- name: prometheus
type: prometheus
url: https://prometheus.example.com/api/v1/write
- name: loki
type: loki
url: https://loki.example.com/loki/api/v1/push

This caused real headaches for teams that:

  • Manage multiple clusters, where you have one common config file that all clusters share and then smaller files that tweak settings for each individual cluster (like different credentials or cluster names). With lists, those smaller files can't change a single property of one destination; they have to redefine the entire list, wiping out everything from the shared file.
  • Deploy with GitOps tools like Argo CD, Terraform, or Flux. To override a single field (say, a password) requires referencing items by their position in the list: --set destinations[0].auth.password=secret. Here, [0] just means "the first item in the list," not "the Prometheus destination." If someone later reorders the list so Loki comes first, that override silently applies to the wrong destination.
  • Split configuration across multiple files. When Helm combines two files that both define the same list, it doesn't merge them together. Instead it throws away the first list and keeps the second. So if your shared file defined two destinations and your per-cluster file only wanted to change a password on one of them, you'd have to redefine both destinations in full or lose the other one entirely.

The solution

In v4, destinations are defined as a map instead of a list. In a list, items are identified by their position (first, second, third). In a map, each item has a unique name that you choose, and that name never changes regardless of what order things appear in.

Here's what the same two destinations look like in v4:

# v4
destinations:
prometheus:
type: prometheus
url: https://prometheus.example.com/api/v1/write
loki:
type: loki
url: https://loki.example.com/loki/api/v1/push

Notice that prometheus and loki are now names at the top level, not items in a numbered list. If you need to override a single property, you refer to the destination by name:

--set destinations.prometheus.auth.password=secret

That path reads clearly ("set the password on the Prometheus destination"), and it won't break if you add, remove, or reorder destinations later. When you split configuration across multiple files, Helm can merge maps together intelligently, combining properties from each file rather than throwing one away.

Collectors

Each collector is an instance of Alloy running in your cluster. You might have one collector scraping metrics, another reading log files, and another handling one-off jobs such as cluster events.

The issue

In v3, the chart came with hard-coded collector names like alloy-metrics, alloy-logs, and alloy-singleton. Each name was tied to a specific deployment type (StatefulSet, DaemonSet, single-replica Deployment). This caused friction when:

  • You were confined by the chart's predefined layout even if you wanted a different topology (like one DaemonSet that handles both logs and metrics).
  • You wanted to add a collector for a new purpose but couldn't because the set of names was fixed.
  • The names didn't tell you how it was deployed, so you had to dig into the docs to understand what you were getting. For example, alloy-metrics: Is this a StatefulSet? Clustered? How many replicas?
  • When you enabled a feature such as clusterMetrics, the chart decided on its own which collector to put it on (for example, routing clusterMetrics to alloy-metrics). You never saw this in your configuration file. That routing was buried in the chart's internal code, so understanding which feature ran where meant reading source code rather than your own config.

The solution

In v4, collectors are a map you define. You pick the names, and you apply one or more presets that describe the deployment shape:

# v4
collectors:
metrics-collector:
presets: [clustered, statefulset]
logs-collector:
presets: [filesystem-log-reader, daemonset]
events-collector:
presets: [singleton]

The chart includes seven presets:

Preset

What it does

clustered

Enables Alloy clustering so replicas share scrape targets

statefulset

Deploys as a StatefulSet

daemonset

Deploys one instance per node

deployment

Deploys as a standard Deployment

singleton

Ensures only a single replica runs

filesystem-log-reader

Mounts the node's `/var/log` directory for reading container log files

privileged

Grants elevated permissions (needed for some profilers)

You can combine multiple presets on a single collector, and their effects stack. For example, [clustered, statefulset] gives you a StatefulSet with Alloy clustering turned on. That's exactly what the old alloy-metrics was configured as internally, but now you can see it clearly in your config instead of needing to look it up.

Features are explicitly assigned to a collector, so there's no hidden magic:

clusterMetrics:
enabled: true
collector: metrics-collector
podLogsViaLoki:
enabled: true
collector: logs-collector

If your setup only has a single collector, you don't need to add collector: to every feature. The chart sees there's only one option and uses it for everything automatically. But if you define two or more collectors, the chart won’t guess which one you want for each feature. If you forget to specify, it will give you a message telling you which feature still needs to be assigned to a collector rather than silently picking one for you.

Telemetry services

Telemetry services are additional applications that run in your cluster to generate monitoring data. For example, Node Exporter collects hardware and OS metrics from your Linux nodes, kube-state-metrics tracks the state of Kubernetes objects like pods and deployments, and OpenCost calculates the cost of running your workloads. These services produce the raw data that your collectors then pick up and send to your destinations.

The issue

In v3, when you enabled a feature like clusterMetrics, this would silently deploy backing services such as Node Exporter, kube-state-metrics, and OpenCost behind the scenes. That was convenient if you were starting from scratch, but caused problems if:

  • Your cluster already had Node Exporter running, and you'd get a duplicate deployment with no warning.
  • You wanted the backing service without the Alloy configuration (or vice versa), but they were inseparable.
  • You wanted fine-grained control over which services were deployed, but the chart bundled them all together under one feature flag.

The solution

In v4, deploying those services is a separate, explicit step under the telemetryServices key:

# v4

telemetryServices:

  kube-state-metrics:

    deploy: true

  node-exporter:

    deploy: true

  opencost:

    deploy: true

If you already have one of these services running in your cluster, you skip the deploy and just point the chart to the existing instance:

telemetryServices:

  node-exporter:

    deploy: false        # don't deploy a new one

hostMetrics:

  enabled: true

  linuxHosts:

    enabled: true

    namespace: monitoring

    labelMatchers:

      app.kubernetes.io/name: prometheus-node-exporter

No more surprise deployments. You can mix-and-match: deploy some services via the chart and reuse others that already exist. The chart's built-in validation will tell you if a feature needs a service you haven't enabled or pointed at yet, so you're never left guessing.

Cluster metrics

Cluster metrics are the numbers that tell you how your Kubernetes cluster is doing: how much CPU and memory your containers are using, how many pods are running or failing, whether nodes are healthy, and so on. These come from sources like the Kubelet (the agent on each node), cAdvisor (which tracks container resource usage), and kube-state-metrics (which reports on the state of Kubernetes objects like Deployments, Jobs, and Pods).

The issue

The v3 clusterMetrics feature was overloaded. When enabled, it configured collection for Kubernetes cluster metrics, Linux host metrics (Node Exporter), Windows host metrics (Windows Exporter), energy metrics (Kepler), and cost metrics (OpenCost), all at once. This caused issues when:

  • You only cared about Kubernetes metrics but didn't want host or cost metrics. You had to hunt through a large values file to disable what you didn't need.
  • You wanted to manage host metrics independently from cluster metrics, but they were tangled together in the same feature.
  • The values file was cluttered with options for all five concerns, making it hard to find the setting you actually needed.

The solution

V4 splits this into three focused features, each with its own values file:

v4 feature

What it covers

clusterMetrics

Kubelet, cAdvisor, kube-state-metrics, control plane

hostMetrics

Linux hosts (Node Exporter), Windows hosts (Windows Exporter), energy (Kepler)

costMetrics

OpenCost (and, in the future, cloud cost exporters)

Each feature's configuration only shows the options relevant to that concern, nothing else. The separation also opens the door for future improvements. For example, a future release could let you choose between Node Exporter and Alloy's built-in host metric collection with a single toggle, potentially eliminating a deployment entirely.

Pod logs 

Pod logs are the text output that your applications write while they're running, like "user logged in," "request failed with error 500," or "database connection timed out." Every container in your cluster produces these logs, and collecting them in a central place is one of the most common reasons to set up monitoring.

The issue

The v3 podLogs feature had a gatherMethod flag that completely changed how it behaved. Depending on the value, you were either collecting logs through Loki's pipeline or through OpenTelemetry's filelog receiver. This was confusing because:

  • The values file mixed configuration for both methods together. Options were littered with notes like "this only applies if gatherMethod is X," making it hard to know which settings were relevant to you.
  • The OpenTelemetry path was needlessly wasteful. It collected logs in OpenTelemetry format, translated them into Loki format, and then translated them back into OpenTelemetry format for delivery. Attributes were lost along the way.
  • Switching between methods meant changing a single flag and hoping the rest of your configuration still made sense for the new method.

The solution

V4 replaces this with two separate features:

v4 feature

Equivalent in v3

podLogsViaLoki

podLogs with the default gather method

podLogsViaOpentelemetry

podLogs with gatherMethod: filelog`

Each feature's values file only shows options that apply to that method. The OpenTelemetry path now delivers logs natively in OTLP format with no round-trip translation, so all original attributes are preserved.

Pod log labels

Every pod in Kubernetes can have labels (like app: frontend or team: payments) and annotations attached to it. When collecting logs, it's useful to carry some of those labels along with each log line so you can later filter or search by them. In v3, labelsToKeep was a configuration option that controlled which of those labels survived the collection process.

The issue

In v3, the pod logs pipeline would take every Kubernetes pod label and annotation and turn them into log labels, then use a labelsToKeep list to filter down to just the ones you wanted. This caused three distinct problems:

  • Customization was painful. If you needed to keep one extra label, you had to redefine the entire default list (about 12 items) and add yours at the end, the same list-override problem that plagued destinations.
  • Memory usage spiked. Alloy allocated labels for every annotation on every pod, potentially hundreds, only to throw most of them away moments later. Users reported their log-collecting Alloy instances struggling with memory, traced directly to this bulk-label behavior.
  • The config was unintuitive. You didn't opt in to labels you wanted; you started with everything and had to figure out which ones to keep.

The solution

In v4, labelsToKeep is removed entirely. Pod annotations and labels are no longer bulk-applied. Instead, you explicitly declare which pod labels and annotations you want promoted to log labels:

podLogsViaLoki:
enabled: true
labels:
- app
- team
annotations:
- release-version

nly the labels you ask for are created. Memory usage drops because Alloy never allocates the ones you don't need. Adding a label is a one-line change, no list redefinition required.

Profiling

Profiling is a way to look inside your running applications to see exactly where they're spending their time and resources. While metrics tell you that something is slow or using too much memory, profiling tells you why, down to the specific function or line of code. The chart supports three types: 

  • eBPF profiling, which uses a Linux kernel feature to observe applications without modifying them
  • Java profiling, which hooks into the Java runtime
  • pprof, which collects profiles from applications that expose a standard profiling endpoint (common in Go applications)

The issue

The v3 profiling feature bundled three profiler types together: eBPF, Java, and pprof. Enabling profiling deployed all of them, even if you only needed one. This was wasteful because:

  • eBPF and Java profilers require elevated permissions and dedicated resources, but you'd get them even if you only wanted lightweight pprof collection.
  • There was no way to run just one profiler without also deploying the others.
  • The profiling collector was a separate, dedicated deployment. You couldn't share it with other features.

The solution

In v4, you enable the profiling feature and then separately enable just the profilers you need:

profiling:

  enabled: true

  ebpf:

    enabled: true

  pprof:

    enabled: true

  java:

    enabled: false

This approach is better in three ways:

  1. The chart only creates the specific labels you listed, so nothing extra gets generated in the first place.
  2. Alloy no longer has to hold hundreds of unused labels in memory just to throw them away, which directly reduces memory consumption. 
  3. If you want to start keeping one more label, you just add a single line to your config instead of having to copy the entire default list and append to it.

Migrating from previous versions

A migration tool is available at grafana.github.io/k8s-monitoring-helm-migrator/. Drop in your current values file and get a v4-compatible values file back. The tool handles the structural changes: converting lists to maps, splitting features, and mapping the old named collectors to the new preset-based system.

All of the chart's examples on the Grafana documentation site and in the repository's examples directory have been updated to reflect the v4 format.

Summary

Every change in v4 follows the same pattern: identify a real pain point (fragile list overrides, hidden deployments, overloaded features, wasted memory) and restructure the chart to eliminate it. 

Ready to get started? Check out the migration tool, browse the updated examples, or dive into the chart documentation.

】

Kubernetes 监控 Helm 图表是将指标、日志、跟踪和配置文件从 Kubernetes 集群发送到 Grafana Cloud（或自托管 Grafana 堆栈）的最简单方法。 4.0 版本是该图表有史以来最大的更新。

它经过近六个月的规划和开发，旨在解决用户随着监控设置的增长而遇到的真正痛点。无论您管理一个集群还是一百个集群，结果都是一个更可预测、更灵活且更易于维护的图表。

如果“Helm 值文件”或“DaemonSet”等术语听起来令人生畏，请不要担心。这篇文章用简单的语言介绍了每一个重大变化，解释了它解决的问题，并展示了实际修复的样子。

目的地

目的地是发送遥测数据的地方，例如用于指标的Prometheus服务器或Loki 日志实例。

问题

在 v3 中，目标被定义为列表：

# v3
目的地：
- 名称：普罗米修斯
类型： 普罗米修斯
网址：https://prometheus.example.com/api/v1/write
- 姓名：洛基
类型：洛基
网址：https://loki.example.com/loki/api/v1/push

这给以下团队带来了真正的麻烦：

  • 管理多个集群，其中您有一个所有集群共享的通用配置文件，然后是调整每个集群设置的较小文件（例如不同的凭据或集群名称）。对于列表，这些较小的文件无法更改一个目标的单个属性；他们必须重新定义整个列表，清除共享文件中的所有内容。
  • 使用 Argo CD、Terraform 或 Flux 等 GitOps 工具进行部署。要覆盖单个字段（例如密码），需要按项目在列表中的位置引用项目：--set destinations[0].auth.password=secret。在这里，[0] 仅表示“列表中的第一项”，而不是“Prometheus 目的地”。如果后来有人重新排序列表，使 Loki 排在第一位，则该覆盖会默默地应用于错误的目标。
  • 将配置拆分到多个文件中。当 Helm 组合两个定义相同列表的文件时，它不会将它们合并在一起。相反，它会丢弃第一个列表并保留第二个列表。因此，如果您的共享文件定义了两个目的地，而您的每个集群文件只想更改其中一个目的地的密码，则您必须完全重新定义这两个目的地，否则就完全丢失另一个目的地。

解决方案

在 v4 中，目的地被定义为地图而不是列表。在列表中，项目按其位置（第一、第二、第三）进行标识。在地图中，每个项目都有一个您选择的唯一名称，并且无论事物以什么顺序出现，该名称都不会改变。

以下是 v4 中相同的两个目的地的样子：

# v4
d目的地：
普罗米修斯：
类型： 普罗米修斯
网址：https://prometheus.example.com/api/v1/write
洛基：
类型：洛基
网址：https://loki.example.com/loki/api/v1/push

请注意，prometheus 和 loki 现在是顶层名称，而不是编号列表中的项目。如果需要覆盖单个属性，请按名称引用目标：

--set destinations.prometheus.auth.password=secret

该路径读起来很清楚（“在 Prometheus 目标上设置密码”），并且如果您稍后添加、删除或重新排序目标，它也不会中断。当您将配置拆分到多个文件时，Helm 可以智能地将映射合并在一起，合并每个文件的属性，而不是丢弃一个文件。

收集器

每个收集器都是在集群中运行的 Alloy 的实例。您可能有一个收集器抓取指标，另一个收集器读取日志文件，另一个处理一次性作业（例如集群事件）。

问题

在 v3 中，图表带有硬编码的收集器名称，例如 alloy-metrics、alloy-logs 和 alloy-singleton。每个名称都与特定的部署类型（StatefulSet、DaemonSet、单副本部署）相关联。这会在以下情况下引起摩擦：

  • 即使您想要不同的拓扑（例如处理日志和指标的一个 DaemonSet），您也会受到图表的预定义布局的限制。
  • 您想要为新目的添加收集器，但由于名称集是固定的而无法实现。
  • 名称没有告诉您它是如何部署的，因此您必须深入研究文档以了解您所得到的内容。例如，alloy-metrics：这是一个 StatefulSet 吗？簇状？有多少个副本？
  • 当您启用 clusterMetrics 等功能时，图表会自行决定将其放在哪个收集器上（例如，将 clusterMetrics 路由到 alloy-metrics）。您从未在配置文件中看到过这一点。该路由隐藏在图表的内部代码中，因此了解哪个功能在哪里运行意味着阅读源代码而不是您自己的配置。

解决方案

在 v4 中，收集器是您定义的地图。您选择名称，然后应用一个或多个描述部署形状的预设：

# v4
收藏家：
指标收集器：
预设：[集群、有状态集]
日志收集器：
预设：[文件系统日志读取器、守护进程集]
事件收集器：
预设：[单例]

该图表包括七个预设：

预设

它的作用

集群

启用 Alloy 集群，以便副本共享抓取目标

statefulset

部署为StatefulSet

daemonset

每个节点部署一个实例

部署

作为标准部署部署

singleton

确保仅运行一个副本

filesystem-log-reader

安装节点的`/var/log`目录以读取容器日志文件

特权

授予提升的权限（某些分析器需要）

您可以组合多个预设在单个收集器上，并且它们的效果叠加。例如，[clustered, statefulset] 为您提供一个启用了 Alloy 集群的 StatefulSet。这正是旧的 alloy-metrics 的内部配置，但现在您可以在配置中清楚地看到它，而无需查找它。

功能已显式分配给收集器，因此没有隐藏的魔力：

clusterMetrics：
启用：真
收集器：指标收集器
podLogsViaLoki：
启用：真
收集器：日志收集器

如果您的设置只有一个收集器，则无需将 collector: 添加到每个功能。该图表显示只有一个选项，并会自动将其用于所有内容。但是，如果您定义两个或多个收集器，图表将无法猜测您想要为每个功能选择哪个收集器。如果您忘记指定，它会向您显示一条消息，告诉您哪些功能仍需要分配给收集器，而不是默默地为您选择一个。

遥测服务

遥测服务是在集群中运行以生成监控数据的附加应用程序。例如，Node Exporter 从 Linux 节点收集硬件和操作系统指标，kube-state-metrics 跟踪 Kubernetes 对象（如 Pod 和部署）的状态，OpenCost 计算运行工作负载的成本。这些服务会生成原始数据，然后收集器会收集这些数据并将其发送到目的地。

问题

在 v3 中，当您启用 clusterMetrics 等功能时，这会在幕后默默部署 Node Exporter、kube-state-metrics 和 OpenCost 等支持服务。如果您从头开始，这很方便，但会在以下情况下引起问题：

  • 您的集群已经运行了 Node Exporter，并且您会得到重复部署且没有任何警告。
  • 您想要没有 Alloy 配置的支持服务（反之亦然），但它们是不可分割的。
  • 您想要对部署的服务进行细粒度控制，但图表将它们全部捆绑在一个功能下

解决方案

在 v4 中，部署这些服务是 telemetryServices 键下的一个单独的显式步骤：

# v4

telemetryServices:

  kube-state-metrics:

    部署： true

  node-exporter:

    deploy: true

  opencost:

    deploy: true

如果您的集群中已运行这些服务之一，则可以跳过部署，只需将图表指向现有服务实例：

遥测服务：

  节点导出器：

    部署： false      �� # 不要部署新的

hostMetrics:

  enabled: true

  linuxHosts:

    enabled: true

    命名空间：监控

    labelMatchers:

      app.kubernetes.io/name: prometheus-node-exporter

不再出现意外部署。您可以混合搭配：通过图表部署一些服务并重用其他已存在的服务。图表的内置验证将告诉您某个功能是否需要您尚未启用或指向的服务，因此您永远不会猜测。

集群指标

集群指标是告诉您 Kubernetes 集群运行情况的数字：您的容器正在使用多少 CPU 和内存、有多少 pod 正在运行或失败、节点是否健康等等。这些数据来自 Kubelet（每个节点上的代理）、cAdvisor（跟踪容器资源使用情况）和 kube-state-metrics（报告 Kubernetes 对象（如部署、作业和 Pod）的状态）等来源。

问题

v3 clusterMetrics 功能过载。启用后，它会一次性配置 Kubernetes 集群指标、Linux 主机指标 (Node Exporter)、Windows 主机指标 (Windows Exporter)、能源指标 (Kepler)、和 成本指标 (OpenCost) 的收集。这会在以下情况下引起问题：

  • 您只关心 Kubernetes 指标，但不需要主机或成本指标。您必须查找大型值文件来禁用不需要的功能。
  • 您希望独立于集群指标来管理主机指标，但它们在同一个功能中纠缠在一起。
  • 值文件中充满了所有五个问题的选项，因此很难找到您实际需要的设置。

解决方案

V4 将其分为三个重点功能，每个功能都有自己的值文件：

v4 功能

涵盖内容

clusterMetrics

Kubelet、cAdvisor、kube-state-metrics、控制平面

hostMetrics

Linux 主机（Node Exporter）、Windows 主机（Windows导出器）、能源（开普勒）

costMetrics

OpenCost（以及将来的云成本导出器）

每个功能的配置仅显示与该问题相关的选项，没有其他内容。这种分离也为未来的改进打开了大门。例如，未来的版本可以让您通过一次切换在 Node Exporter 和 Alloy 的内置主机指标集合之间进行选择，从而可能完全消除部署。

Pod 日志

Pod 日志是您的应用程序在运行时写入的文本输出，例如“用户登录”、“请求失败并出现错误 500”或“数据库连接超时”。集群中的每个容器都会生成这些日志，并将它们收集在一个中心位置是设置监控的最常见原因之一。

问题

v3 podLogs 功能有一个 gatherMethod 标志完全改变了它的行为方式。根据该值，您可以通过 Loki 的管道或通过 OpenTelemetry 的文件日志接收器收集日志。这很令人困惑，因为：

  • 值文件将两种方法的配置混合在一起。选项中充斥着“这仅适用于 GatherMethod 为 X 的情况”之类的注释，因此很难知道哪些设置与您相关。
  • OpenTelemetry 路径毫无必要地浪费了。它收集 OpenTelemetry 格式的日志，将其转换为 Loki 格式，然后将其转换回 OpenTelemetry 格式以进行交付。在此过程中属性丢失了。
  • 在方法之间切换意味着更改单个标志，并希望其余配置对于新方法仍然有意义。

解决方案

V4 将其替换为两个单独的功能：

v4 功能

等效于v3

podLogsViaLoki

podLogs 使用默认收集方法

podLogsViaOpentelemetry

podLogs 使用gatherMethod: filelog`

每个功能的值文件仅显示适用于该方法的选项。 OpenTelemetry 路径现在以 OTLP 格式原生提供日志，无需往返转换，因此所有原始属性都被保留。

Pod 日志标签

Kubernetes 中的每个 pod 都可以附加标签（例如 app: frontend 或 team: payment）和注释。收集日志时，在每个日志行中携带一些标签非常有用，以便您以后可以通过它们进行过滤或搜索。在 v3 中，labelsToKeep 是一个配置选项，用于控制哪些标签在收集过程中幸存下来。

问题

在 v3 中，pod 日志管道将获取每个 Kubernetes pod 标签和注释，并将它们转换为日志标签，然后使用 labelsToKeep 列表过滤出您想要的标签。这导致了三个不同的问题：

  • 定制是痛苦的。如果您需要保留一个额外的标签，则必须重新定义整个默认列表（大约 12 个项目）并在末尾添加您的列表，这与困扰目的地的列表覆盖问题相同。
  • 内存使用量激增。 Alloy 为每个 Pod 上的每个注释分配了标签，可能有数百个，但很快就扔掉了其中的大部分。用户报告说他们的日志收集 Alloy 实例在内存方面遇到困难，直接追溯到这种批量标签行为。
  • 该配置不直观。您没有选择加入您想要的标签；您从一切开始，并且必须弄清楚要保留哪些。

解决方案

在 v4 中，labelsToKeep 被完全删除。 Pod 注释和标签不再批量应用。相反，您可以显式声明要升级为日志标签的 pod 标签和注释：

podLogsViaLoki：
已启用：真实
标签：
- 应用程序
- 团队
注释：
- 发布版本

仅创建您要求的标签。内存使用量下降是因为 Alloy 永远不会分配您不需要的内存。添加标签是单行更改，无需重新定义列表。

分析

分析是一种查看正在运行的应用程序内部的方法，以准确了解它们在哪里花费了时间和资源。虽然指标告诉您某件事速度缓慢或使用了太多内存，但分析则告诉您原因，具体到特定的函数或代码行。该图表支持三种类型：

  • eBPF 分析，它使用 Linux 内核功能来观察应用程序而不对其进行修改
  • Java 分析，它挂钩到 Java 运行时
  • pprof，它从公开标准分析端点的应用程序收集配置文件（在 Go 应用程序中常见）

问题

捆绑的 v3 分析 功能三种分析器类型：eBPF、Java 和 pprof。启用分析会部署所有这些，即使您只需要一个。这是一种浪费，因为：

  • eBPF 和 Java 分析器需要提升的权限和专用资源，但即使您只需要轻量级 pprof 收集，您也可以获得它们。
  • 无法在不部署其他分析器的情况下只运行一个分析器。
  • 分析收集器是单独的专用部署。您无法与其他功能共享它。

解决方案

在 v4 中，您启用分析功能，然后单独启用您需要的分析器：

分析：

  启用：true

  ebpf：

    启用： true

  pprof:

    enabled: true

  java:

    enabled: false

此方法在三个方面更好：

  1. 图表仅创建您列出的特定标签，因此首先不会生成任何额外的内容。
  2. Alloy 不再需要在内存中保存数百个未使用的标签只是为了将它们丢弃，这会直接将其丢弃。减少内存消耗。 
  3. 如果您想开始保留一个以上标签，只需在配置中添加一行，而不必复制整个默认列表并附加到其中。

从以前的版本迁移

可以在 grafana.github.io/k8s-monitoring-helm-migrator/。放入当前的值文件并获取 v4 兼容的值文件。该工具可处理结构更改：将列表转换为地图、拆分要素以及将旧的命名收集器映射到新的基于预设的系统。

Grafana 文档站点 和 存储库的示例目录已更新以反映 v4 格式。

摘要

v4 中的每个更改都遵循相同的模式：识别真正的痛点（脆弱列表覆盖、隐藏部署、超载功能、浪费内存）并重组图表以消除它。 

准备好开始了吗？查看迁移工具，浏览更新的示例，或深入了解图表文档。

item.title: 【Business metrics in Grafana Cloud: Get an AI assist to help securely analyze your data】Grafana Cloud 中的业务指标：获得 AI 协助以帮助安全地分析您的数据
item.description:
【

For today's modern businesses, the data landscape demands security and flexibility. 

You need to connect your observability platform to rich, proprietary datasets that often reside in private networks without compromising security or managing complex network infrastructure. You may also face an extra layer of complexity in order to effectively query and visualize that data. Luckily, modern artificial intelligence tools have made these previously complicated processes much simpler.

This is where Grafana Cloud's private data source connect (PDC) truly shines, offering a secure, elegant solution to bring relational data like business metrics and analytics directly into your dashboards. This secure connection also allows Grafana Assistant to access the data and leverage the power of AI to visualize and query your data.

In this blog post, we’ll demonstrate how you can access that private data securely in Grafana Cloud and how to use our AI assistant to translate complex database queries into human readable language and visualizations.

Making business analytics easy with PDC, Assistant, and Postgres

Observability was born from the need to give engineers deeper visibility into their workloads, but the scope of how it's used is quickly expanding. In fact, half of all organizations today use observability tools to track business-related metrics such as security, compliance, revenue, order tracking, customer conversions, and more, according to our 2026 Observability Survey.

So while Grafana Cloud was built by engineers for engineers, it's also powerful and flexible enough to meet a wide range of needs, including business analytics. In this section, we'll briefly describe the tools you'll need to get started and tell you a little bit about the data source we'll use to demo this functionality.

The power of PDC

PDC is a key feature for enterprise-grade observability. It establishes a secure, encrypted, private connection between your Grafana Cloud instance and data sources hosted within your private networks.

Here's how it works: A lightweight PDC agent is deployed in your private network. This agent creates a customer-controlled SSH tunnel back to Grafana Cloud, securely routing all queries. This critical design choice means:

  • Security first: Your databases are never exposed to the public internet. Traffic is encrypted end-to-end.
  • Simplicity: You avoid the complexity of managing VPNs, NAT gateways, or intricate network-level access controls.
  • Scalability: The agent can be deployed for high availability and easily scaled to meet your query demands.
  • Local experience: You configure the data source in Grafana as if it were running locally within your private network.

PostgreSQL: analytics beyond metrics

While tools like Prometheus are essential for scraping and querying time series metrics from infrastructure and applications, many critical business insights live in relational databases. PostgreSQL, with its robust support for complex queries, joins, and rich datasets, is the perfect complement to pure metrics-based observability.

Consider the example of the World Happiness Report, which is a research-based global report that ranks countries by how happy their people say they are, and explores the social and economic factors behind those differences. This dataset is full of relational context: countries, years, GDP per capita, life expectancy, and social support. Visualizing this data requires sophisticated queries that are not easily performed using traditional metrics-optimized sources.

By connecting PostgreSQL via PDC, you can:

  • Query relational data like business metrics, customer survey results, or rich time series data
  • Perform complex joins to enrich time-series metrics with contextual data
  • Unlock deep analytics directly within your Grafana dashboards

Grafana Assistant: Query the data with natural language 

Grafana Assistant is our LLM purpose-built for Grafana Cloud. It's an invaluable AI-powered feature that significantly accelerates the dashboard creation process, as it lets you leverage natural language prompts to generate complex queries and refine visualizations quickly. 

In this demo, Grafana Assistant was used to rapidly construct and fine-tune the prebuilt dashboard, demonstrating how it can quickly turn raw PostgreSQL data into meaningful, happiness-focused visualizations.

AI-powered dashboard generation from PostgreSQL

When connecting to a rich data source like PostgreSQL via PDC, Assistant acts as an intelligent translator between your analytical goal and the necessary SQL.

Here's how Assistant works with the PostgreSQL data source:

  1. Natural Language query translation: Instead of manually writing complex SQL joins and aggregations, a user can simply prompt the assistant. For example: "Show me the trend of 'Life Ladder' score over time for the top 5 happiest countries in 2024."
  2. SQL generation: The AI processes this prompt, understands the structure of the connected PostgreSQL schema (e.g., table names, column names like Life Ladder, country_name, year), and automatically generates the precise SQL query required to fetch the data.
  3. Visualization suggestion and refinement: Once the query runs, Assistant analyzes the returned dataset (e.g., time series data, categorical rankings). It then suggests the most appropriate visualization type (e.g., time series panel for trends, bar chart for rankings) and generates the panel configuration, including axis labels and legends.

This capability drastically lowers the barrier to entry for users who may not be SQL experts, allowing them to rapidly prototype and deploy complex analytical dashboards based on their private relational data.

Automated setup: A Terraform blueprint for secure observability

To demonstrate this modern observability pattern, we've created a comprehensive Terraform repository that automates the entire setup. This blueprint embodies the principle of "infrastructure as code" for your secure data connections.

Everything you need to set things up can be found in this public GitHub repo: https://github.com/mwimpelberg28/grafana_happiness

The blueprint includes the following components:

  • Amazon RDS PostgreSQL instance: Provisioned securely within a private Amazon VPC, preloaded with the World Happiness Report dataset
  • PDC agent deployment: The PDC agent is deployed within the same private VPC to establish the secure tunnel and enforce network restrictions
  • Grafana Terraform provider: Used to programmatically create the secure PostgreSQL data source, configured specifically to route queries over the PDC tunnel
  • Prebuilt Grafana dashboard: A ready-to-use dashboard featuring PostgreSQL queries to visualize happiness data, including:
    • Time series panels tracking happiness scores over time
    • Bar charts ranking countries by key metrics
    • Tables correlating happiness metrics with factors like GDP per capita and life expectancy

Getting started with PDC and PostgreSQL

Before you start deploying the Terraform setup, you need to configure the connection credentials within Grafana Cloud. This involves setting up an access policy and a service account to authenticate the PDC agent.

Setup instructions

1. Find the cluster your Grafana stack is deployed in. You will use this as the value of the pdc_cluster Terraform variable.

2. Create a new service account with the Admin role within Grafana. In your Grafana Cloud instance, navigate to Administration > Users and access > Service accounts and then click Add service account.

Once the service account is created, click Add service account token. Copy the token and set it as the value of the sa_token Terraform variable.

3. Next, create a new access policy by navigating to Administration > Users and access > Cloud access policies, then click Create access policy. Name your policy and then add the following permissions:

You will need to click Add scope in order to add the stacks:read permission. After you’ve created the policy, click Add token, name it (and optionally set an expiration date), then copy the token to the cloud_access_policy_token Terraform variable.

Now that we’ve finished setting up connection credentials in Grafana, let’s set the remaining Terraform variables:

Variable

Example value

grafana_url

https://.grafana.net

grafana_slug

vpc_name

happiness-demo-vpc

5. Now you are ready to provision the infrastructure needed to support the demo. From the grafana_happiness repository directory, run terraform init to download the project’s dependencies, then terraform apply to create the infrastructure and Grafana resources. Please be aware that provisioning the necessary infrastructure and resources can frequently take eight minutes or more.

6. Once terraform apply has completed successfully you will find a new dashboard at Dashboards > Happiness > World Happiness Index:

Now you are visualizing data from your new private data source using PDC!

7. This demo deploys real cloud infrastructure that costs money, so remember to run terraform destroy when you are done exploring so you don’t incur unwanted expenses.

What's next

We encourage you to use the provided demo as a starting point for your business analytics journey with Grafana Cloud. And now that you are connected to your private network, you can use any of the supported data sources for Grafana Assistant to help you analyze and visualize your business data. 

Assistant can run ad hoc queries, build dashboards, and help gain insight into data without having to learn numerous query languages. In addition, Assistant can also provide the translated SQL queries if you need to use them in other systems. And check out this guide for even more use cases to explore in your journey with Grafana Assistant.

】

对于当今的现代企业来说，数据环境需要安全性和灵活性。 

您需要将可观测平台连接到通常驻留在专用网络中的丰富的专有数据集，而不会影响安全性或管理复杂的网络基础设施。为了有效地查询和可视化该数据，您可能还面临额外的复杂性。 幸运的是，现代人工智能工具使这些以前复杂的过程变得更加简单。

这就是 Grafana Cloud 的私有数据源连接 (PDC) 真正发挥作用的地方，为将业务指标和分析等关系数据直接引入您的仪表板。这种安全连接还允许 Grafana Assistant 访问数据并利用 AI 的力量来可视化和查询您的数据。

在这篇博文中，我们将演示如何在 Grafana Cloud 中安全地访问私有数据，以及如何使用我们的 AI 助手将复杂的数据库查询转换为人类可读的语言和可视化。

使用 PDC、Assistant 和 Postgres 使业务分析变得轻松

可观察性源于为工程师提供更深入了解的需要。其工作负载的可见性，但其使用范围正在迅速扩大。事实上，根据我们的2026 年可观测性调查，如今有一半的组织使用可观测性工具来跟踪与业务相关的指标，例如安全性、合规性、收入、订单跟踪、客户转化率等。

因此，虽然 Grafana Cloud 是由工程师为工程师构建的，但它也足够强大和灵活，可以满足包括业务在内的广泛需求。分析。在本节中，我们将简要介绍您入门所需的工具，并向您介绍一些我们将用于演示此功能的数据源。

PDC 的强大功能

PDC 是企业级可观察性的一项关键功能。它在您的 Grafana Cloud 实例和专用网络中托管的数据源之间建立安全、加密的专用连接。

其工作原理如下：在您的专用网络中部署轻量级 PDC 代理。该代理创建一条返回 Grafana Cloud 的客户控制的 SSH 隧道，安全地路由所有查询。这一关键的设计选择意味着：

  • 安全第一：您的数据库永远不会暴露在公共互联网上。流量进行端到端加密。
  • 简单性：您可以避免管理 VPN、NAT 网关或复杂的网络级访问控制的复杂性。
  • 可扩展性：可以部署代理以实现高可用性，并轻松扩展以满足您的查询需求。
  • 本地体验：您在 Grafana 中配置数据源，就好像它在您的专用网络中本地运行一样。

PostgreSQL：超越指标的分析

而像 Prometheus 对于从基础设施和应用程序中抓取和查询时间序列指标至关重要，许多关键业务洞察都存在于关系数据库中。 PostgreSQL 凭借对复杂查询、联接和丰富数据集的强大支持，是对纯粹基于指标的可观察性的完美补充。

以世界幸福报告为例，这是一份基于研究的全球报告，根据各国人民的幸福程度对各国进行排名，并探讨这些差异背后的社会和经济因素。该数据集充满了相关背景：国家、年份、人均 GDP、预期寿命和社会支持。可视化这些数据需要使用传统的指标优化源不容易执行的复杂查询。

通过 PDC 连接 PostgreSQL，您可以：

  • 查询业务指标、客户调查结果或丰富的时间序列数据等关系数据
  • 执行复杂的联接，以利用上下文数据丰富时间序列指标
  • 直接在 Grafana 中解锁深度分析仪表板

Grafana Assistant：使用自然语言查询数据

Grafana Assistant是我们专为 Grafana Cloud 构建的法学硕士。这是一项宝贵的人工智能功能，可显着加速仪表板创建过程，因为它允许您利用自然语言提示来生成复杂的查询并快速完善可视化。 

在此演示中，Grafana Assistant 用于快速构建和微调预构建的仪表板，演示如何快速将原始 PostgreSQL 数据转化为有意义的、以幸福为中心的可视化效果。

从 PostgreSQL 生成人工智能驱动的仪表板

通过 PDC 连接到 PostgreSQL 等丰富数据源时，Assistant 充当您的分析目标和必要 SQL 之间的智能转换器。

以下是 Assistant 如何与PostgreSQL数据源：

  1. 自然语言查询翻译：用户无需手动编写复杂的SQL连接和聚合，只需提示助手即可。例如：“显示 2024 年排名前 5 个最幸福国家的‘Life Ladder’分数随时间变化的趋势。”
  2. SQL 生成：AI 处理此提示，了解连接的 PostgreSQL 架构的结构（例如，表名称、列名称，如 Life Ladder、country_name、year），并自动生成精确值获取数据所需的 SQL 查询。
  3. 可视化建议和细化：查询运行后，Assistant 会分析返回的数据集（例如时间序列数据、分类排名）。然后，它会建议最合适的可视化类型（例如，趋势的时间序列面板，栏图表排名）并生成面板配置，包括轴标签和图例。

此功能大大降低了非 SQL 专家的用户的进入门槛，使他们能够根据自己的私有关系数据快速原型化和部署复杂的分析仪表板。

自动化设置：安全可观测性的 Terraform 蓝图

为了演示这种现代可观测性模式，我们创建了一个全面的 Terraform自动化整个设置的存储库。此蓝图体现了安全数据连接的“基础设施即代码”原则。

设置所需的所有内容都可以在此公共 GitHub 存储库中找到：https://github.com/mwimpelberg28/grafana_happiness

该蓝图包括以下组件：

  • Amazon RDS PostgreSQL 实例：在私有 Amazon VPC 中安全配置，预加载了世界幸福报告数据集
  • PDC代理部署：PDC代理部署在同一私有VPC内，以建立安全隧道并实施网络限制
  • Grafana Terraform提供商：用于以编程方式创建安全的PostgreSQL数据源，专门配置为通过PDC隧道路由查询
  • 预构建的Grafana仪表板：一个随时可用的仪表板，具有PostgreSQL查询功能，可可视化幸福数据，包括：
    • 跟踪一段时间内幸福感得分的时间序列面板
    • 按关键指标对国家/地区进行排名的条形图
    • 将幸福感指标与人均 GDP 和预期寿命等因素相关联的表格

开始使用 PDC 和 PostgreSQL

在开始部署 Terraform 设置之前，您需要在 Grafana Cloud 中配置连接凭据。这涉及设置访问策略和服务帐户以对 PDC 代理进行身份验证。

设置说明

1.找到部署 Grafana 堆栈的集群。您将使用它作为 pdc_cluster Terraform 变量的值。

2.在 Grafana 中创建一个具有管理员角色的新服务帐户。在您的 Grafana Cloud 实例中，导航至管理> 用户并访问>服务帐户，然后单击添加服务帐户。

创建服务帐户后，单击添加服务帐户令牌。复制令牌并将其设置为 sa_token Terraform 变量的值。

3.接下来，导航到管理>用户和访问>云访问策略，创建新的访问策略，然后单击创建访问策略。为您的策略命名，然后添加以下权限：

您需要单击添加范围才能添加stacks:read 权限。创建策略后，点击添加令牌，为其命名（并可以选择设置到期日期），然后将令牌复制到 cloud_access_policy_token Terraform 变量。

现在我们已经完成在 Grafana 中设置连接凭据，让我们设置其余的 Terraform 变量：

变量

示例值

grafana_url

https://.grafana.net

grafana_slug

vpc_name

happiness-demo-vpc

5.现在您已准备好配置支持演示所需的基础设施。从 grafana_happiness 存储库目录中，运行 terraform init 下载项目的依赖项，然后运行 ​​terraform apply 创建基础设施和 Grafana 资源。请注意，配置必要的基础设施和资源通常需要八分钟或更长时间。

6. terraform apply 成功完成后，您将在仪表板>幸福感 >世界幸福指数中找到一个新仪表板：

现在您可以 使用 PDC 可视化来自新私有数据源的数据！

7.此演示部署真正的云基础设施需要花钱，因此请记住在完成探索后运行 terraform destroy，以免产生不必要的费用。

下一步

我们鼓励您使用提供的演示作为 Grafana Cloud 业务分析之旅的起点。现在您已连接到专用网络，您可以使用 Grafana Assistant 支持的任何数据源来帮助您分析和可视化业务数据。 

Assistant 可以运行即席查询、构建仪表板并帮助深入了解数据，而无需学习多种查询语言。此外，如果您需要在其他系统中使用它们，Assistant 还可以提供翻译后的 SQL 查询。请查看本指南，了解更多用例，供您探索我们与 Grafana Assistant 一起工作。

item.title: 【Query fair usage in Grafana Cloud: What it is and how it affects your logs observability practice】Grafana Cloud 中的查询公平使用：它是什么以及它如何影响您的日志可观察性实践
item.description:
【

In Grafana Cloud we use a simple yet generous formula that lets you query up to 100x your monthly ingested log volume in gigabytes for free. This works for the vast majority of our customers, but if you aren’t careful and strategic with your usage, you could find yourself with an overage bill. 

We obviously don't want to surprise any customer with an unexpected bill, so in this blog post you'll learn how to find your usage ratio in your Grafana dashboard so you can understand what you're looking at, where the queries are coming from, and who your top users are. You'll also learn some best practices for applying good policies to your observability practice going forward.

What is 'query fair usage' and why does the policy exist?

In Grafana Cloud Logs, "query fair usage” refers to a pricing policy that lets you query up to 100x your monthly ingested log volume in GBs at no additional charge. It is primarily a billing mechanism designed to allow typical usage without extra charges, while preventing abuse from extremely resource-intensive queries.

Grafana Cloud’s query engine (especially for Loki logs) can scan huge amounts of data. Without guardrails, a few heavy queries could create disproportionate load. The fair‑use policy:

  • Keeps costs predictable
  • Encourages efficient querying
  • Protects shared infrastructure
  • Still allows generous exploration of logs

 Example

If you ingest 50 GB of logs in a month:

  • Your fair‑use query allowance = 50 GB × 100 = 5,000 GB of queried logs.

As long as your queries scan ≤ 5,000 GB, you pay nothing extra for queries.

How to find and monitor your query fair usage

How to view query usage

For starters, you can actually view the cost of a query before you run a query. When you write queries in Explore, Grafana provides an estimate of how much data will be queried when you run your query.

But if you want to know your usage ratio, you have a few options. The first way, and the way most people would be aware of, is by going to the billing dashboard to view your current query ratio.

  • From the Grafana main menu, click the dashboard icon.
  • Select the Billing/Usage dashboard.
  • Scroll down to the Logs Ingestion and Query Details section.
  • Expand the section and scroll to the Query Usage Ratio panel.

The screenshot below is an example of what you might see. This is from a large environment with seven different Grafana instances.

Another way to view your usage ratio is by utilizing the newly redesigned Cost Management and billing experience. This is actually the way you should get familiar with viewing your usage, as the billing dashboard has been deprecated and will be removed some time in the future.

Once you're there, simply scroll down to Products, and locate and expand Logs. From there go to the bottom of the page where you will find the log query rate as well as the query usage ratio.

Determine the source of query usage

To help you track down the source of query usage, we built the Loki query fair usage dashboard. For Grafana Cloud customers using hosted Grafana, this dashboard is automatically provisioned on each of your hosted Grafana instances.

The dashboard shows a breakdown of your query usage, query type, dashboard, grafana-alert, and Explore/other, by query bytes and query count. For each type, there are rows showing more detailed information on highest volume queries, including:

  • The originating query
  • The Grafana username that submitted the query (if relevant)
  • Rule and dashboard names
  • Query size in bytes
  • Query execution frequency

And here's a breakdown of the different panels in the dashboard:

  • Grafana-alerts refers to rules managed within Grafana under Grafana Alerting found at Home > Alerts & IRM > Alerting. These rules can be alerts or recording rules. These are separate from the rules you upload to Loki with cortextool or lokitool using the Grafana Cloud APIs.
  • Explore/other refers to a subset of queries executed against Loki that come from the Explore page. It doesn’t include those coming from the Grafana Logs Drilldown app. It also includes queries that come from a non-Grafana frontend source such as logcli. Explore queries likely have a grafana_username populated in the dashboard, queries from other sources don’t.
  • Estimated interval is the estimation of how frequently the query ran over the selected time period. It’s the number of times the query ran divided by the total time range.

Understanding your Grafana Cloud invoice for logs

At this point, you have learned what query fair usage is, where to find it in your billing dashboard, and how to determine the source of your query usage. Now let's get an understanding of how that could impact your monthly invoice. 

Grafana Cloud calculates logs usage by looking at the following components:

  • GBs ingested: The total number of GBs ingested into Grafana Cloud on a monthly basis.
  • GBs retained: How long the logs data are retained within Grafana Cloud.

The minimum retention period is 30 days and you can purchase additional retention periods in 30-day increments. Retention is customizable per stack or by individual streams within the same stack. To enable this, contact Grafana Support.

Note: The retention period changes are not retroactive. Once the retention is increased, the current logs will be stored following the new retention period, but logs already out of the old retention period will not be recovered.

Billing calculations

Billing is based on usage, and usage is determined by these primary factors:

  • The number of GBs ingested per month
  • The number of months of retention

Note: For customers exceeding the 100x fair use policy for GBs queried per month, the following billing calculations apply:

logs billable gb = max(ingested gb, queried gb / fair use query ratio)

This calculation is performed on a per-stack level.

Even though you can see what your query fair usage is at any time, you are only billed for it at the beginning of the month for your previous month. Oftentimes early in the billing cycle, your usage ratio will be high, but as the month goes on, it often drops as more logs are being sent in.

Example

  • Ingested: 50 GB
  • Queried: 7,000 GB
  • Fair‑use threshold: 5,000 GB

Billable GB = max(50, 7,000 / 100) = max(50, 70) = 70 GB

So you would be billed for 70 GB instead of 50 GB at whatever your set rate is per GB in your Grafana contract.

Managing your query fair usage costs

Now that we've walked through the usage policy and how it could impact your costs, let's finish with some tips for avoiding potential overages in the future

Recommendations

One common source of excess queries is misconfigured Grafana/Loki-managed alerting rules—for example, querying one hour of data but running that query every minute.

For alerting rules using the Loki data source:

  • Use instant queries instead of range queries for all rules. An instant query executes exactly one time and produces one data point for each series matched by your label selectors. Range queries are effectively instant queries executed multiple times. For more details, refer to the How to run faster Loki metric queries with more accurate results blog post.
  • Look at the evaluation period and interval period and make their intervals match the amount of time queried. That is, a rule that runs once per minute should have a query range of 1m.

Note: We also recommend checking alert rules run by the scheduler (recorded queries).

Best practices for fair query usage

To stay within fair usage policies and optimize performance:

  • Filter early: Use label selectors and log pipeline filters at the beginning of your queries (e.g., in LogQL) to reduce the data set before applying more complex operations.
  • Avoid wide scans: Be cautious with queries that scan large time ranges or entire datasets, especially when using Grafana Assistant.
  • Narrow time ranges: Select a smaller time frame instead of querying “last 30 days” by default.
  • Use aggregation and recording rules: Define Prometheus recording rules to pre-calculate frequently used, resource-heavy expressions into new metrics. Querying these pre-aggregated metrics is much more efficient than calculating them ad-hoc.
  • Avoid wildcard-heavy filters: They force Loki to scan massive amounts of data, which will consume your query usage quickly. A precise label selector like {cluster="us-central1"} can reduce the search space from, say, 100 TB down to 1 TB.
  • Use labels wisely: Loki’s indexing model rewards good label design. For more details on how to do this, check out our concise guide to labels in Loki.
  • Optimize alert rules: Ensure your log-based alert rules follow best practices, as they are a common cause of excessive query usage. And for more information on how to address poorly defined alert rules, check out our docs page on this topic.
